The Six-Layer Infrastructure Stack","item":"https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-agentic-operating-system"}]}</script> <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org","@type":"FAQPage","mainEntity":[{"@type":"Question","name":"What's the difference between an agentic OS and a multi-agent system?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"A multi-agent system is a collection of AI agents working together. An agentic OS is the infrastructure that makes multi-agent systems work reliably over time — the memory, orchestration, shared context, and workflow management on top of the agents themselves. You can have a multi-agent system without an agentic OS. You just won't be able to run it at scale."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"Do you need to be technical to build an agentic OS?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Not entirely, but some technical depth helps. The hardest parts — memory infrastructure, orchestration logic, integration with external tools — require understanding how the components fit together. The easier parts — defining shared context, designing workflows at a conceptual level, setting up monitoring — don't require coding. Most practical implementations are collaborative: someone technical builds the infrastructure, and a domain expert defines the business logic."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"How is an agentic OS different from a chatbot?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"A chatbot responds to questions. An agentic OS runs processes. A chatbot has no persistent memory, no ability to take action in the world, no coordination with other systems. An agentic OS maintains state across sessions, executes multi-step workflows, coordinates multiple specialized agents, and operates proactively. The comparison is roughly like asking how a spreadsheet is different from a full accounting system."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"What's the minimum viable agentic OS?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"You need at least: one or more agents with defined capabilities, some form of persistent memory (even just a shared document with business context), a workflow that chains at least two steps, and a way to monitor outputs. That's it. You can build on top of that incrementally. The business brain pattern — shared context that all agents read from — is often the highest-leverage starting point because it immediately improves quality across everything."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"Can an agentic OS replace employees?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Specific roles that are primarily task execution — data entry, basic research, formatting, scheduling, routine communications — can be largely automated within an agentic OS. Roles that require judgment, client relationships, strategic decisions, and creative direction remain human. The more realistic outcome is that one person can do the work of several, because the routine execution happens autonomously. Think of it as removing the administrative burden from knowledge work rather than replacing knowledge workers."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"What tools are commonly used to build an agentic OS?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Common components include: LLMs like Claude or GPT-4 for reasoning, vector databases or memory platforms for persistence, orchestration frameworks, and integration layers for connecting to business tools. For the custom interfaces and dashboards needed to manage the system, spec-driven development tools like Remy can significantly speed up the build. There's no single off-the-shelf agentic OS product — it's assembled from components, which is both the challenge and the flexibility."}}]}</script><link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.fontshare.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://api.fontshare.com/v2/css?f[]=gambarino@400&display=swap"> <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap"> <link rel="stylesheet" href="/_astro/Base.DsKTEypW.css">

 if (toggle) toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');

 if (closeBtn) closeBtn.addEventListener('click', closeDrawer);

 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = isOpen ? '' : 'rotate(180deg)';
 });
 });

 // Dropdown menus

 const btn = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-toggle]');
 const menu = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-menu]');

 });
 });
 });

The Six-Layer Infrastructure Stack" class="ms-blog-hero-img" width="1200" height="675" loading="eager"> <div class="prose prose-lg max-w-none ms-blog-content">  <h2 id="the-problem-with-running-a-business-on-disconnected-ai-tools">The Problem with Running a Business on Disconnected AI Tools</h2>
<p>Most businesses using AI in 2026 are doing it the same way: one chatbot for customer questions, another prompt for writing copy, a separate workflow for data analysis. Each tool works in isolation. None of them share context. None of them remember what happened last Tuesday. None of them coordinate with each other.</p>
<p>That’s not a business brain. That’s a pile of calculators.</p>
<p>An <strong>agentic operating system</strong> — or agentic OS — is the architectural answer to this problem. It’s the layer that connects memory, tools, agents, and workflows so AI can run business processes end-to-end, autonomously, without starting from scratch every time.</p>
<p>This article explains what an agentic OS actually is, how it differs from traditional automation or standalone AI tools, what its core components look like, and how businesses are starting to build one.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="what-an-agentic-os-actually-is">What an Agentic OS Actually Is</h2>
<p>An agentic operating system isn’t software you install. It’s a structural pattern — a way of organizing AI agents, memory systems, tools, and workflows so they function as a coordinated whole rather than isolated parts.</p>
<p>Think of how a computer’s operating system works. It doesn’t do the work itself. It manages resources, routes tasks to the right processes, maintains state across sessions, and makes sure programs can communicate with each other. An agentic OS does the same thing for AI-driven business operations.</p>
<p>At its core, an agentic OS has three jobs:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Maintain context</strong> — Store what the business knows, what clients need, what’s happened before, and what the current state of any project is.</li>
<li><strong>Coordinate agents</strong> — Route tasks to the right agent or skill, chain outputs together, and manage multi-step workflows.</li>
<li><strong>Take autonomous action</strong> — Run processes proactively, not just when a human asks.</li>
</ol>

<p>This is fundamentally different from <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-agentic-ai">agentic AI</a> in its simple form. A single agent responding to prompts is useful. An agentic OS makes multiple agents useful <em>together</em>, over time, across clients and projects.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="how-it-differs-from-traditional-automation">How It Differs from Traditional Automation</h2>
<p>Traditional automation tools — Zapier, Make, even rule-based RPA — are trigger-response machines. If X happens, do Y. They’re rigid, they don’t reason, and they can’t handle ambiguity.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agentic-workflows-vs-traditional-automation">Agentic workflows are fundamentally different</a>. They can evaluate conditions, branch based on what they find, loop until a goal is met, and make decisions mid-process. That’s not automation in the old sense. It’s more like delegating to someone who can actually think through a problem.</p>
<p>The agentic OS takes this further by adding:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Persistent memory</strong> across sessions and agents</li>
<li><strong>Shared context</strong> that all agents in the system can access</li>
<li><strong>Orchestration logic</strong> that decides which agent handles which task</li>
<li><strong>Proactive scheduling</strong> rather than waiting for human triggers</li>
</ul>
<p>A Zapier workflow runs when triggered. An agentic OS runs because it knows what needs to happen — sometimes before you’ve thought to ask.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="the-core-components-of-an-agentic-os">The Core Components of an Agentic OS</h2>
<h3 id="memory">Memory</h3>
<p>Memory is what separates a useful system from a useless one. Without it, every agent interaction starts cold. You have to re-explain the client, the brand voice, the project status, the constraints.</p>
<p>An agentic OS typically uses two types of memory:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Long-term memory</strong> — Stored facts about the business, clients, processes, preferences. This persists across sessions and across agents.</li>
<li><strong>Working memory</strong> — The context window for the current task. What’s been decided, what’s pending, what the current output looks like.</li>
</ul>
<p>The <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-agent-memory-wall-long-running-jobs">AI agent memory wall</a> is one of the most common failure points in real deployments. When an agent runs a long task and exhausts its context window, it loses track of what it was doing. A well-designed agentic OS solves this with external memory stores that agents can read from and write to at any point in a workflow.</p>
<p>Research from Mem0, a dedicated agent memory infrastructure provider, shows that purpose-built memory systems significantly outperform model-native memory solutions on complex, multi-turn tasks — something worth considering when designing your memory layer.</p>
<h3 id="tools-and-integrations">Tools and Integrations</h3>
<p>Agents are only as useful as the tools they can reach. An agentic OS needs to connect to the software your business actually runs on — CRMs, project management tools, communication platforms, databases, file systems.</p>
<p>This is harder than it sounds. Most businesses use a mix of SaaS tools that weren’t designed to be accessed by AI. <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agent-integration-layer-compose-io-enterprise-tools">The integration layer problem</a> — connecting N agents to M tools without building N×M custom integrations — is one of the key architectural challenges when building an agentic OS.</p>
<p>The practical solution is to use a unified integration layer that abstracts tool connections into standardized APIs that any agent can call.</p>
<h3 id="orchestration">Orchestration</h3>
<p>Who decides which agent handles which task? That’s orchestration. It’s the traffic controller of the agentic OS.</p>

<p>Without orchestration, you get <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agent-sprawl-microservices-problem-ai-teams">agent sprawl</a> — dozens of agents that nobody has a clear picture of, running in isolation, duplicating work, or conflicting with each other. The microservices problem that plagued software teams a decade ago is showing up again in AI deployments, just faster.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agent-orchestration-biggest-unsolved-problem-ai-stack">Agent orchestration</a> handles task routing, sequencing, and handoffs between agents. It decides whether a task should be handled by a single specialized agent or broken into subtasks distributed across multiple agents. It monitors for failures and decides what to do when something goes wrong.</p>
<h3 id="workflows">Workflows</h3>
<p>Individual agents handle discrete tasks. Workflows chain those tasks into business processes.</p>
<p>A content production workflow might look like: research a topic → draft an outline → write a draft → apply brand guidelines → run a quality check → send for approval. Each step is handled by a different agent or skill. The workflow is what connects them — passing outputs from one to the next, branching when conditions are met, looping when revisions are needed.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agentic-workflows-explained-conditional-logic-branching">Agentic workflows with conditional logic, loops, and branching</a> are what make this possible. It’s not a linear script. It’s a process that adapts based on what it encounters.</p>
<h3 id="shared-context">Shared Context</h3>
<p>This is the “business brain” part. Shared context is the structured information that all agents in the system can access: brand voice, client preferences, standard operating procedures, product knowledge, historical decisions.</p>
<p>Without shared context, each agent operates in a vacuum. The copy agent doesn’t know what the sales agent quoted the client. The research agent doesn’t know what the client already rejected last quarter.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/shared-brand-context-vs-context-folder-ai-memory-layers">The two memory layers every AI system needs</a> — shared brand context and project-specific context folders — are what give the whole system coherence. Agents don’t just execute tasks; they execute them <em>correctly</em> because they know the relevant background.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="what-the-business-brain-pattern-looks-like-in-practice">What the Business Brain Pattern Looks Like in Practice</h2>
<p>The “business brain” is a specific implementation pattern within an agentic OS. It’s the idea that your business’s core knowledge — who you are, what you do, how you communicate, what your clients need — lives in a structured, accessible form that every agent can draw on.</p>
<p>Here’s what that looks like in a real deployment:</p>
<p><strong>Global context</strong> contains things like:</p>
<ul>
<li>Brand voice guidelines</li>
<li>Core service offerings and pricing</li>
<li>Standard client personas</li>
<li>Company history and values</li>
<li>Legal or compliance constraints</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>Client context</strong> contains:</p>
<ul>
<li>Individual client history</li>
<li>Preferences and communication style</li>
<li>Active project status</li>
<li>Past decisions and outcomes</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>Process context</strong> contains:</p>
<ul>
<li>Standard operating procedures for each workflow</li>
<li>Quality standards and checklists</li>
<li>Escalation rules</li>
</ul>
<p>When an agent starts a task, it loads the relevant context layers before it does anything else. It doesn’t ask you to re-explain who the client is. It already knows.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/shared-business-brain-claude-code-skills">Building a shared business brain for your AI skills</a> is one of the highest-leverage things you can do early in building an agentic OS. It’s what makes the system feel like it actually understands your business rather than just completing tasks.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="multi-agent-architecture-how-skills-chain-into-workflows">Multi-Agent Architecture: How Skills Chain Into Workflows</h2>
<p>A mature agentic OS isn’t one big agent. It’s a collection of specialized agents — sometimes called “skills” — that each do one thing well, and a coordination layer that chains them together.</p>

<p>This is the <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/claude-code-skill-collaboration-chaining-workflows">skill collaboration pattern</a>: individual skills that are modular, testable, and composable. A research skill, a writing skill, a QA skill, a formatting skill. Each one is narrow enough to be reliable. The workflow chains them into something powerful.</p>
<p>The benefits of this architecture:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Reliability</strong> — Narrow agents fail in predictable ways and are easier to debug.</li>
<li><strong>Reusability</strong> — The same research skill can be used in multiple workflows.</li>
<li><strong>Scalability</strong> — You can add new capabilities by adding new skills without rebuilding everything.</li>
<li><strong>Parallel execution</strong> — Multiple skills can run simultaneously when tasks don’t depend on each other.</li>
</ul>
<p>For teams managing multiple clients or projects, this matters enormously. The same workflow engine that handles one client’s content calendar can handle twenty, with different context loaded for each.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="the-proactive-layer-heartbeats-and-autonomous-scheduling">The Proactive Layer: Heartbeats and Autonomous Scheduling</h2>
<p>Most AI tools wait to be asked. An agentic OS doesn’t have to.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agentic-os-heartbeat-pattern-proactive-ai-agent">The heartbeat pattern</a> is what keeps agents running proactively. A heartbeat is a scheduled trigger — every morning, every hour, every time a condition is met — that kicks off work without human input.</p>
<p>Practical examples:</p>
<ul>
<li>An agent checks your CRM every morning, flags deals that have gone quiet for more than five days, and drafts follow-up emails for your review.</li>
<li>An agent monitors your client’s social channels, detects when a competitor launches something, and queues a briefing.</li>
<li>An agent reviews all open project tasks every Sunday, identifies blockers, and sends a prioritized week-ahead summary.</li>
</ul>
<p>This is <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/post-prompting-era-proactive-ai-agents">the shift from reactive to proactive AI</a> — and it’s what separates an agentic OS from a collection of prompt-based tools. You’re not managing prompts. You’re managing goals.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="managing-an-agentic-os-goals-not-terminals">Managing an Agentic OS: Goals, Not Terminals</h2>
<p>One of the least-discussed challenges in building an agentic OS is how you actually manage it day to day. Early approaches involved watching terminal outputs, reading logs, manually checking agent states. That doesn’t scale.</p>
<p>The shift that makes an agentic OS manageable is <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/managing-ai-agents-by-goals-not-terminals">managing agents by goals, not terminals</a>. Instead of asking “what is this agent doing right now?” you ask “is this goal being met?” You set objectives, monitor outcomes, and intervene when results are off — not when processes are running.</p>
<p>This requires a command center — a single interface where you can see the status of all running agents, the goals they’re working toward, the outputs they’ve produced, and the interventions needed.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agentic-os-command-center-manage-agents-by-goals">The agentic OS command center</a> is what makes this practical. It abstracts away the implementation details and gives you a goal-oriented view of your AI operations.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="the-infrastructure-stack-behind-it">The Infrastructure Stack Behind It</h2>
<p>An agentic OS sits on top of a broader infrastructure stack. Understanding the layers helps you see what you’re actually building and what you need.</p>
<p>According to practitioners in the field, <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/agent-infrastructure-stack-six-layers-explained">the agent infrastructure stack</a> typically includes:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Model layer</strong> — The AI models doing the reasoning</li>
<li><strong>Memory layer</strong> — Where context and history are stored</li>
<li><strong>Tool/integration layer</strong> — What the agents can actually do in the world</li>
<li><strong>Orchestration layer</strong> — What coordinates multi-agent workflows</li>
<li><strong>Workflow layer</strong> — The processes that chain tasks together</li>
<li><strong>Interface layer</strong> — How humans interact with and monitor the system</li>
</ol>
<div class="not-prose"><div class="v3-frame v3-frame-b03" data-v3-variant="hr02" data-v3-angle="educational"><div class="hr-ad-image"><img class="hr-ad-img" src="https://i.mscdn.ai/1b7301c0-de42-4e46-b110-e9c55396e7ca/generated-images/957ba1d7-9c1c-417e-97da-2a97eac46966.png?fm=auto&w=1200" alt="Hermes Crash Course — free 1-hour live workshop" loading="lazy" decoding="async"></div><div class="hr-ad-bar"><img class="hr-ad-logo" src="https://i.mscdn.ai/images/76b81a63-51ec-43f4-b4ea-0b5023be600a_1780006013492.png?fm=auto&w=200" alt="Hermes" width="80" height="23" loading="lazy"><span class="hr-ad-tagline">The free Hermes Agent crash course</span><a class="hr-ad-cta" href="https://luma.com/remy-sbqx" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-cta="reserve-hermes-workshop" data-cta-location="blog-interstitial" data-cta-format="b03-02" data-cta-angle="educational" data-cta-copy-index="0" data-cta-variant-id="b03-02">Reserve your spot <span class="hr-ad-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span></a></div></div></div>
<p>Most early agentic OS deployments handle the model layer and maybe the workflow layer. The memory and orchestration layers get bolted on later — which creates technical debt and reliability problems. Building those in from the start is significantly easier than retrofitting them.</p>

<h2 id="common-mistakes-when-building-an-agentic-os">Common Mistakes When Building an Agentic OS</h2>
<h3 id="starting-with-too-many-agents">Starting with too many agents</h3>
<p>The instinct is to build specialized agents for everything. The reality is that each agent adds coordination overhead. Start with the fewest agents needed to cover your core workflows. Add more as you identify specific capability gaps.</p>
<h3 id="skipping-memory-architecture">Skipping memory architecture</h3>
<p>Running agents without persistent memory means they’re stateless. Every workflow starts cold. This is fine for simple, one-off tasks but breaks down immediately for anything ongoing. Build your memory layer early, even if it’s simple.</p>
<h3 id="no-shared-context">No shared context</h3>
<p>Agents that don’t share context produce incoherent outputs. Your copy agent shouldn’t have to infer brand voice from first principles every time. The <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/context-layer-ai-most-valuable-asset">context layer is one of the most valuable assets</a> in your AI system — treat it that way.</p>
<h3 id="managing-by-process-instead-of-by-outcome">Managing by process instead of by outcome</h3>
<p>If you’re spending time watching what agents are doing rather than what they’re producing, the architecture is wrong. Design your monitoring around outcomes and exception handling, not process visibility.</p>
<h3 id="under-investing-in-workflow-design">Under-investing in workflow design</h3>
<p>Individual agents are relatively easy to get right. Workflows that chain agents together reliably are much harder. Spend time designing the handoffs, the error handling, the quality gates. <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-wat-framework-workflows-agents-tools">The WAT framework — Workflows, Agents, and Tools</a> — is a useful mental model for thinking through what you actually need at each layer.</p>

<p>A multi-agent system is a collection of AI agents working together. An agentic OS is the infrastructure that makes multi-agent systems work reliably over time — the memory, orchestration, shared context, and workflow management on top of the agents themselves. You can have a multi-agent system without an agentic OS. You just won’t be able to run it at scale.</p>
<h3 id="do-you-need-to-be-technical-to-build-an-agentic-os">Do you need to be technical to build an agentic OS?</h3>
<p>Not entirely, but some technical depth helps. The hardest parts — memory infrastructure, orchestration logic, integration with external tools — require understanding how the components fit together. The easier parts — defining shared context, designing workflows at a conceptual level, setting up monitoring — don’t require coding. Most practical implementations are collaborative: someone technical builds the infrastructure, and a domain expert defines the business logic.</p>
<h3 id="how-is-an-agentic-os-different-from-a-chatbot">How is an agentic OS different from a chatbot?</h3>
<p>A chatbot responds to questions. An agentic OS runs processes. A chatbot has no persistent memory, no ability to take action in the world, no coordination with other systems. An agentic OS maintains state across sessions, executes multi-step workflows, coordinates multiple specialized agents, and operates proactively. The comparison is roughly like asking how a spreadsheet is different from a full accounting system.</p>
<h3 id="whats-the-minimum-viable-agentic-os">What’s the minimum viable agentic OS?</h3>
<p>You need at least: one or more agents with defined capabilities, some form of persistent memory (even just a shared document with business context), a workflow that chains at least two steps, and a way to monitor outputs. That’s it. You can build on top of that incrementally. The business brain pattern — shared context that all agents read from — is often the highest-leverage starting point because it immediately improves quality across everything.</p>
<h3 id="can-an-agentic-os-replace-employees">Can an agentic OS replace employees?</h3>
<p>Specific roles that are primarily task execution — data entry, basic research, formatting, scheduling, routine communications — can be largely automated within an agentic OS. Roles that require judgment, client relationships, strategic decisions, and creative direction remain human. The more realistic outcome is that one person can do the work of several, because the routine execution happens autonomously. Think of it as removing the administrative burden from knowledge work rather than replacing knowledge workers.</p>
<h3 id="what-tools-are-commonly-used-to-build-an-agentic-os">What tools are commonly used to build an agentic OS?</h3>
<p>Common components include: LLMs like Claude or GPT-4 for reasoning, vector databases or memory platforms for persistence, orchestration frameworks, and integration layers for connecting to business tools. For the custom interfaces and dashboards needed to manage the system, spec-driven development tools like <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy">Remy</a> can significantly speed up the build. There’s no single off-the-shelf agentic OS product — it’s assembled from components, which is both the challenge and the flexibility.</p>
<hr>
<h2 id="key-takeaways">Key Takeaways</h2>
<ul>
<li>An <strong>agentic operating system</strong> connects memory, tools, orchestration, and workflows so AI agents can run business processes end-to-end and autonomously.</li>
<li>The core components are <strong>memory</strong> (persistent context across sessions), <strong>tools</strong> (integrations with external systems), <strong>orchestration</strong> (coordination between agents), <strong>workflows</strong> (multi-step process chains), and <strong>shared context</strong> (the business brain that all agents draw from).</li>
<li>It’s fundamentally different from traditional automation, which is trigger-based and rigid. An agentic OS reasons, adapts, and operates proactively.</li>
<li>The biggest practical mistakes are skipping memory architecture, running agents without shared context, and managing by process instead of by outcome.</li>
<li>Building the custom interfaces, dashboards, and tools an agentic OS requires doesn’t have to take months — spec-driven development with <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy">Remy</a> can get you to a working, full-stack system significantly faster.</li>

        var links = document.querySelectorAll('a[href]');
        links.forEach(function (link) {
          var href = link.getAttribute('href');
          if (!href || href.startsWith('#') || href.startsWith('mailto:') || href.startsWith('tel:') || href.startsWith('javascript:')) return;

          try {
            var url = new URL(href, window.location.origin);
            params.forEach(function (value, key) {
              if (!url.searchParams.has(key)) {
                url.searchParams.set(key, value);
              }
            });

            if (href.startsWith('/') || href.startsWith('./') || href.startsWith('../') || !href.includes('://')) {
              link.setAttribute('href', url.pathname + url.search + url.hash);
            } else {
              link.setAttribute('href', url.toString());
            }
          } catch (e) {}
        });
      });
    </script> <!-- A/B Testing: Inject experiment assignments --> <script>(function(){const experimentAssignments = "{}";

      window.__msExperiments = JSON.parse(experimentAssignments);
    })();</script> <!-- A/B Testing: Fire Experiment View events --> <script>
      if (window.__msExperiments && typeof window.plausible === 'function') {
        Object.keys(window.__msExperiments).forEach(function (expId) {
          window.plausible('Experiment View', {
            props: {
              experiment: expId,
              variant: window.__msExperiments[expId],
              page: window.location.pathname,
            },
          });
        });
      }
    </script> <!-- Plausible Custom Events: CTA Clicks + Scroll Depth --> <script>
      (function () {
        // CTA Click tracking — fires on elements with data-cta attribute
        document.addEventListener('click', function (e) {
          var el = e.target.closest('[data-cta]');
          if (!el || typeof window.plausible !== 'function') return;
          var ctaName = el.getAttribute('data-cta');
          if (!ctaName) return;
          var ctaProps = {
            cta_name: ctaName,
            cta_location: el.getAttribute('data-cta-location') || 'unknown',
            page: window.location.pathname,
          };
          var ctaFormat = el.getAttribute('data-cta-format');
          if (ctaFormat) ctaProps.cta_format = ctaFormat;
          var ctaAngle = el.getAttribute('data-cta-angle');
          if (ctaAngle) ctaProps.cta_angle = ctaAngle;
          var ctaVariantId = el.getAttribute('data-cta-variant-id');
          if (ctaVariantId) ctaProps.cta_variant_id = ctaVariantId;
          var ctaCopyIndex = el.getAttribute('data-cta-copy-index');
          if (ctaCopyIndex) ctaProps.cta_copy_index = ctaCopyIndex;
          var ctaSlotIndex = el.getAttribute('data-cta-slot-index');
          if (ctaSlotIndex) ctaProps.cta_slot_index = ctaSlotIndex;
          if (window.__msExperiments) {
            Object.keys(window.__msExperiments).forEach(function (expId) {
              ctaProps['exp_' + expId.replace(/-/g, '_')] = window.__msExperiments[expId];
            });
          }
          window.plausible('CTA Click', { props: ctaProps });
        });

        // Scroll Depth tracking — fires at 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% milestones
        var fired = {};
        var thresholds = [25, 50, 75, 100];
        function checkScroll() {
          var docHeight = document.documentElement.scrollHeight - window.innerHeight;
          if (docHeight <= 0) return;
          var pct = Math.round((window.scrollY / docHeight) * 100);
          for (var i = 0; i < thresholds.length; i++) {
            var t = thresholds[i];
            if (pct >= t && !fired[t] && typeof window.plausible === 'function') {
              fired[t] = true;
              window.plausible('Scroll Depth', {
                props: { depth: t + '%', page: window.location.pathname },
              });
            }
          }
        }
        window.addEventListener('scroll', checkScroll, { passive: true });
        // Check once on load for short pages
        window.addEventListener('load', checkScroll);
      })();
    </script> <!-- Vercel Web Analytics --> <script defer="" src="/_vercel/insights/script.js"></script>  </body></html>
